petitioner_background: The petitioner seeks classification of the beneficiary as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts as a musician (drummer) under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. The beneficiary is a Jamaican reggae and gospel musician who was a founding member of a reggae band (referred to inconsistently in the record). He is described in support letters as a drummer, composer, arranger, and music tutor who worked with the Flames of Glory gospel music group and the Bee Zee Street Music Academy. The Director of the Vermont Service Center denied the petition, finding the petitioner had not established the sustained national or international acclaim required for the classification.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Several support letters claimed the beneficiary and his band won awards, but the letters provided inconsistent descriptions. One letter stated the band received the 'Rockers Award for the Band with the Best Original Style' in 1986 and 1987, and the 'Martin International Award for the Best Reggae Band' in 1987. Another stated the band won 'many Awards Including International Awards; Rockers Awards for the [sic] Band for the year 1991.' Other letters referenced the Rockers Award and Martin International Award without providing dates, categories, or details. Counsel stated on appeal that the beneficiary received two Rockers Awards and the Martin International Award, claiming the Rockers Award is 'akin to the American Grammy Awards' and a prestigious regional awards show in the Caribbean, and that the Martin International Award is a coveted prize within the Reggae music community. No primary evidence (such as pictures of the awards or award documentation) was submitted. Counsel stated 'Pictures of the awards are not available but several letters which were submitted testify to the Beneficiary's having been so awarded.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the support letters provided inconsistent descriptions of the alleged awards regarding titles, dates, and subject areas. Counsel's unsupported assertions about the prestige and recognition of the awards do not constitute evidence, citing Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez. The petitioner failed to submit any primary evidence of the awards and failed to resolve the inconsistencies in the record with independent objective evidence, citing Matter of Ho. The record contained no documentation of the significance and national or international recognition of the awards. Accordingly, the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel claimed the beneficiary meets this criterion through his membership in the Jamaica Federation of Musicians and Affiliated Artistes' Union. A letter from the President of the Union affirmed the beneficiary's membership in the Union, but provided no description of the Union's membership criteria.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record contained no evidence that the Union requires outstanding achievements of its members as judged by recognized national or international experts in the field. Accordingly, the beneficiary did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: With the Form I-140, the petitioner submitted no evidence of published materials about the beneficiary's work. On the Form I-290B, counsel stated the petitioner did submit 'evidence of published material in major media publications about the Beneficiary,' but on appeal counsel made no mention of published materials and submitted no evidence of such materials.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Since no evidence of published materials about the beneficiary was submitted, the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel claimed that without the beneficiary's original contributions to Reggae and Gospel music, these musical forms would be different today. Counsel cited four recommendation letters: (1) the President of the Jamaica Federation of Musicians and Affiliated Artistes Union stating the beneficiary is a reputed drummer and music tutor whose services are in demand in Jamaica and overseas; (2) a musician and broadcaster in Jamaica describing the beneficiary as one of the top drummers in Jamaica with exceptional gifts for composing, arranging, and writing music, noting his diverse musical background and work with the Flames of Glory gospel music group; (3) an entertainment journalist praising the beneficiary as a musician and citing his success with the band and his work with the Bee Zee Street Music Academy; and (4) similar letters from a manager/bassist, managing director of Bare Essentials, and Chief Executive Officer of Great Gospel Musical Productions Limited in Jamaica. Additionally, the petitioner submitted two compact discs and six recording labels listing the petitioner as a musician, producer, arranger, or composer. The compact discs and one recording label were undated; the remaining recording labels were dated between 1992 and 1997, with four produced in Jamaica. One letter stated the beneficiary is featured on an album with 'Friends' plus three singles including 'Jammin On the [...]' and 'Pickney.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that while the letters praised the beneficiary's skills and described his musical career in Jamaica, they did not state that any of his accomplishments constituted original artistic contributions of major significance to his field. Letters from independent experts solicited in support of an immigration petition carry less weight than preexisting, independent evidence of major contributions expected of an alien who has sustained national or international acclaim. The petitioner submitted no evidence that the beneficiary's recordings were critically acclaimed, a commercial success, or otherwise significantly influenced others. The record was devoid of documentary evidence corroborating the beneficiary's claimed achievements. Moreover, the beneficiary's most recent documented recording was in 1997, seven years before the petition was filed, so the work does not demonstrate the requisite sustained acclaim. Accordingly, the beneficiary did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Several recommendation letters stated the beneficiary performed at numerous concerts and festivals in Jamaica, including Heineken Startime, Reggae Sumfest, Sting, Reggae Ram Jam, Hot Shot, Reggae Sunsplash, Reggae Carnival, Border Clash, Bob Marley Birthday Bash, White River Reggae Bash, and Knox Salute. One letter further stated the band performed at unspecified venues and on unspecified dates in Europe, the United Kingdom, Canada, Japan, and the United States.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Although this criterion generally applies to the visual arts, the AAO addressed the references to performances. The AAO noted that frequent performances are inherent to the musical profession, and the regulation requires evidence demonstrating sustained national or international acclaim rather than simply documenting continued employment. The record contained no documentation of any of the beneficiary's performances or evidence that any of the festivals or concerts mentioned in the support letters are equivalent to major artistic exhibitions or showcases. Accordingly, the beneficiary did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel claimed the beneficiary meets this criterion because he was a founding member of the band, performed with famous musicians, and appeared at esteemed musical festivals in Jamaica.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record was devoid of any primary evidence of the beneficiary's work with the band. The petitioner submitted no evidence of the band's purported recordings, awards, or performances. The record did not establish that the band was critically acclaimed, had a major influence on other musicians in Jamaica or abroad, or otherwise enjoyed a distinguished reputation. The record was similarly devoid of documentation of performances with famous musicians or at the festivals cited. The petitioner also failed to submit evidence regarding the significance, national or international recognition, or distinguished reputation of any of the musical festivals, and the record did not document the beneficiary's allegedly leading or critical role in any distinguished musical group. Accordingly, the beneficiary did not meet this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, concluding that the petitioner failed to establish extraordinary ability through extensive documentation of sustained national or international acclaim demonstrating that the beneficiary has risen to the very top of his field as a musician. The beneficiary did not meet any of the regulatory criteria discussed, and the record contained only inconsistent letter testimony unsupported by primary or corroborating evidence. The AAO also noted counsel's procedural error arguing the director should have issued a Request for Evidence, explaining that 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(8) does not require an RFE where initial evidence supports denial, and remand would serve no useful purpose since counsel submitted only previously-submitted documents on appeal. The burden of proof remains with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act, and the petitioner did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted two oversights by counsel on appeal. First, counsel's brief incorrectly referred to the beneficiary as being in the 'professional soccer community' and being recruited for a coaching staff, which was inapposite given the beneficiary is a musician, not an athlete or coach. Second, counsel contended the director should have issued a Request for Evidence (RFE) and referenced responding to an RFE with newspaper articles, contracts, and translations — but the director did not issue an RFE in this case and the record contained no such response or evidence. The AAO also found inconsistencies in the record regarding the alleged awards that the petitioner failed to resolve with independent objective evidence.
